name: Electrogravitics Research
description: Electrogravitics, also known as electrohydrodynamics (EHD) or electrokinetics, refers to a theoretical concept and purported area of research exploring the interaction between electromagnetic fields and gravity. Proponents claim that it is possible to manipulate gravitational fields using high-voltage electric fields, potentially leading to anti-gravity or advanced propulsion technologies. This idea gained prominence in the mid-20th century, particularly in the United States, fueled by rumors of secret government research and technological breakthroughs. The core of the theory often involves the Biefeld-Brown effect, an observed phenomenon where an asymmetrical capacitor generates thrust when subjected to a high voltage. This effect is typically explained by mainstream physics as ion drift and electrostatic forces acting on the surrounding air, not a direct manipulation of gravity. Claims of antigravity or gravity control based on electrogravitics are widely dismissed by the scientific community due to a lack of reproducible evidence and a strong reliance on misinterpretations of established physics. Despite this, the concept continues to capture the imagination and inspires amateur research and speculative technological development. The American context is central due to the alleged involvement of US military and aerospace companies in secret projects, further fueling the conspiracy theories surrounding the topic. The lack of transparency and declassified information contribute to the enduring mystery and the persistence of electrogravitics in fringe science circles.
subtitle: Manipulating Gravity with Electricity?

claims.claim: Gravity can be manipulated using high-voltage electric fields.
claims.evidence: Biefeld-Brown effect experiments showing thrust generation.
claims.criticism: Thrust is due to ion drift and electrostatic forces, not gravity manipulation. No reproducible evidence of gravity alteration.

claims.claim: Secret US government projects have developed anti-gravity technology based on electrogravitics.
claims.evidence: Declassified documents, witness testimonies, and alleged leaked information.
claims.criticism: Lack of verifiable evidence; documents often misinterpreted; witness accounts unreliable; claims contradict established physics.

claims.evidence: Similarities between observed UFO movements and theoretical electrogravitic propulsion systems.
claims.criticism: Based on speculation and unverified UFO sightings. No concrete evidence linking UFOs to electrogravitics.

keyFigures.name: Thomas Townsend Brown
keyFigures.role: Early experimenter and proponent of electrogravitics.

keyFigures.name: Paul LaViolette
keyFigures.role: Author and proponent of electrogravitics and related theories.

keyFigures.name: Glen A. Robertson
keyFigures.role: Scientist who has critiqued electrogravitics claims

mythologyConnections: Flying carpets and other levitating objects in mythology represent ancient knowledge of electrogravitics.
Connections to Vimana technology described in ancient Indian texts, suggesting advanced aerial vehicles powered by similar principles.

timeline.event: Thomas Townsend Brown begins experimenting with high-voltage capacitors and observes the Biefeld-Brown effect.
Project Winter Haven and Project Y, alleged US military research into electrogravitics.
Increased interest in electrogravitics within fringe science and online communities.

sources.author: Thomas Townsend Brown
sources.work: Various patents and articles on electrostatics and electrogravitics
sources.title: Thomas Townsend Brown
